title: What AlexNet Added
concept: AlexNet was not just 'a CNN on ImageNet' — it combined a deeper network, ReLU activations, GPU training, dropout, and data augmentation so deep visual features could actually train and generalize at ImageNet scale.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why was ImageNet especially useful for a model like AlexNet?

options.text: It had enough labeled variety to reward models that learn rich features at scale
It only contained one kind of object

exercises.explain: Right — a bigger, broader dataset made it worthwhile to train a more ambitious CNN.

cards.title: AlexNet was a recipe, not one trick
cards.body: AlexNet did not win because of one magic layer. It won because several design choices worked together: more depth, faster activations, GPU compute, regularization, and more varied training views of each image.

cards.title: What AlexNet added
cards.body: Compared with older CNNs, AlexNet used a deeper stack (five convolutional layers plus three fully connected layers), ReLU activations instead of slower saturating choices, GPU training for speed, dropout to reduce overfitting, and data augmentation like crops and flips to create more varied examples.

cards.title: Training camp with better gear
cards.body: Imagine a team preparing for a tournament. A deeper playbook gives more strategy, faster practice tools let you train more rounds, and scrimmaging from different angles keeps you from memorizing one exact setup. AlexNet's choices played those roles for a CNN.

cards.title: How those choices help on real images
cards.body: Suppose the task is recognizing dog breeds. Early layers can learn edges and fur textures, middle layers can learn ears or snouts, and later layers can combine those parts into breed-level clues. Random crops and flips help the network avoid memorizing one exact dog pose, while dropout keeps the final classifier from leaning too hard on a few neurons.

cards.title: More layers alone were not enough
cards.body: A common myth is that AlexNet proved depth always wins automatically. In reality, deeper models are harder to train. ReLU helped learning move faster, GPUs made that training practical, and dropout plus augmentation helped the model generalize instead of just memorizing.

cards.title: Older CNN habits vs. AlexNet's recipe
cards.body: AlexNet combined several helpful choices instead of relying on just one.

left.text:
Shallower networks
Older nonlinearities
Less practical large-scale training

exercises.prompt: Which bundle of ideas best matches what AlexNet added?

options.text: Deeper CNN layers, ReLU activations, GPU training, dropout, and data augmentation
No activations, no training, and only hand-written edge rules
A single linear layer with no regularization
Only more labels, with the architecture unchanged

exercises.explain: Exactly — AlexNet's importance comes from that package of design choices, not one isolated trick.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Dropout and data augmentation both help reduce overfitting, but they do it in different ways.
exercises.explain: True — dropout regularizes the network internally, while augmentation makes the training examples more varied.

exercises.sentence: AlexNet used ___ activations, which helped training move faster than older saturating choices.
exercises.explain: Correct — ReLU was one of AlexNet's important training-friendly choices.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best explains why AlexNet could use more depth effectively?

options.text: It paired depth with ReLU, GPUs, dropout, and augmentation instead of relying on extra layers alone
It removed training and guessed categories from file names
It solved overfitting by showing each image only once

exercises.explain: Right — AlexNet's added tricks helped make a deeper CNN practical and useful.

exercises.prompt: A student says, 'So AlexNet was just a bigger LeNet, right?' Which reply is best?

options.text: Not quite — it was deeper, but it also used ReLU activations, GPU training, dropout, and data augmentation so the larger model could train and generalize on ImageNet
Yes — the only change was renaming the layers
No — AlexNet stopped using convolution and switched to only manual rules
Yes — it won mainly because benchmarks became easier in 2012

exercises.explain: Exactly. AlexNet mattered because it combined several training and regularization ideas into one scalable CNN recipe. Next: why that recipe beat older hand-crafted vision pipelines.
